==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Taikodani Inari Shrine''' (太皷谷稲成神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsuwano Town]], [[Kanoashi District]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is classified as a special shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁) and was formerly designated as a village shrine (郷社). It is commonly referred to as "Tsuwano's Oinari-san" and is sometimes considered one of the [[Three Great Inari Shrines]] of Japan, though it is more commonly recognized as one of the [[Five Great Inari Shrines]]. It ranks second in the number of annual visitors in Shimane Prefecture, following [[Izumo Taisha]].
== History == The shrine was founded on May 15, 1773 (An'ei 2) by the 7th lord of the Tsuwano Domain, Kamei Norisada, who invited deities from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for the prosperity of the domain and its people. The shrine was initially built on the ridge of Taikodani, which is the kimon (northeast) direction of Tsuwano Castle, considered an unlucky direction. In July 1867 (Keiō 3), the Tsuwano Domain conducted a shrine reorganization and moved the Kumano Gongen Shrine, which was enshrined on Otomeyama, to the Taikodani Inari Shrine as an auxiliary deity, renaming it Kumano Shrine. The shrine received reverence from successive lords, and it was not open to the public until after the abolition of feudal domains.
In 1923 (Taishō 12), the shrine pavilion (now the Gongen) was built. In 1924 (Taishō 13), treasures were donated by Princess Tomoko of the Kitashirakawa-no-miya Imperial Family, leading to the establishment of the treasure hall and Yōrō Library. The shrine was renamed back to Taikodani Inari Shrine in 1927 (Shōwa 2), and it was designated as a village shrine in 1935 (Shōwa 10). With the increase in visitors, a new shrine pavilion and main hall were constructed and dedicated in 1969 (Shōwa 44), with the old shrine remaining as the Gongen.
The shrine claims to be one of the Five Great Inari Shrines, alongside [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], [[Takekoma Inari Shrine]], and [[Yutoku Inari Shrine]].
== Shrine Name == The shrine is located in a valley where taiko drums used to sound during the Edo period, giving it the name "Taikodani." The unique spelling "稲成" (instead of the usual "稲荷") symbolizes the granting of wishes. It is the only shrine in Japan to use this particular kanji combination for Inari.
== Deities == * [[Ukanomitama|Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami]] (Inari Ōkami) * [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] (Kumano Ōkami)
[[File:View_from_Taikodani_Inari-jinja_shrine,_Tsuwano-cho,_Kanoashi-gun,_Shimane_001.jpg|thumb|500x500px|View of Tsuwano Town from the shrine grounds.]] == Grounds == * Front Approach - The approach consists of 263 stone steps lined with about 1,000 red torii gates. * Chōzuya (Purification Pavilion) * Shrine Gate - A triple gate established in December 1972 (Shōwa 47). The central gate is only used during the New Year and for special festivals. * Main Pavilion and Worship Hall - The new pavilion where it is customary to offer fried tofu. * Rear Worship Hall - Located behind the main pavilion, providing a closer place for worship. * Gongen - The former main pavilion before the new pavilion was built in 1969. * Myōbu Shrine - A subsidiary shrine located behind the Gongen, dedicated to the deity's fox attendants. * Office * Treasure Hall - Houses 88 special treasures, 77 paintings and calligraphy works, 58 pieces of fine art, and 16 other items. * Wedding Hall * Ceremony Hall - A multipurpose hall that can be used for receptions and other events.
<gallery> File:Toriis of Taikodani Inari Shrine 4.jpg|alt=|Front approach with a thousand torii gates File:Shimmon Gate of Taikodani Inari Shrine.jpg|alt=|Shrine gate (approach side) File:Taikodani Inari-jinja.jpg|alt=|Shrine gate (inside grounds) File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Offerings 001.jpg|alt=|Offerings of fried tofu </gallery>
== Subsidiary Shrines == * Gongen ** Deities: Izanami-no-Mikoto (Kumano Ōkami), Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami (Inari Ōkami) :: The former main pavilion before the new pavilion was built in 1969, now serving as the Gongen with a divided spirit enshrined. * Myōbu Shrine ** Deities: Myōbu Senjo - Fox attendants of Inari Ōkami, known as the "Ojou" couple. :: The term "Myōbu" refers to a court lady of the Heian period with a fifth rank or higher, and it is also an epithet for fox attendants of Inari Ōkami.
<gallery> File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Former Shrine 001.jpg|alt=|Gongen File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Myōbu Auxiliary Shrine 001.jpg|alt=|Myōbu Shrine File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Myōbu Auxiliary Shrine 002.jpg|alt=|Myōbu Shrine Pavilion </gallery>
== Festivals == The following festivals are held annually.
Monthly Festivals * 1st and 15th of each month - "Otsuitachi Mairi" and the monthly festival of the former Kumano Shrine.
Annual Festivals * February - First Horse Festival * March - Old First Horse Festival * May 15 - Spring Festival * November 15 - Annual Festival * November 16 - Autumn Festival * November 23 - Harvest Festival
== Cultural Properties == === Shimane Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === ; Old Documents * Celestial Globe and Terrestrial Globe - Designated on May 31, 1966 :: Made by Hori Kinsuke in 1808 (Bunka 5). * Colored maps of Japan's geographical survey and the coastal survey map of the 33 provinces of Eastern Japan - Designated on December 27, 1974 :: Copies of maps surveyed by Inō Tadataka, made by Takahashi Kageyasu.
== Shrine Treasures == The treasure hall houses 88 special treasures, 77 paintings and calligraphy works, 58 pieces of fine art, and 16 other items (currently, armor worn by Kamei Norisada is exhibited at the Kamei Historical Museum).
* Exhibits in the Treasure Hall ** 'Portrait of Kamei Norimasa hunting a tiger' (by Kurimoto Kakuza) ** 'Gift letter for the tiger' (by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) ** 'Four-leaf sakaki crest armor' (worn by retainer Arai Shichibei) ** 'Tiles from Shikano Castle' (bearing the Kamei family crest) ** 'Silkworm Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Portrait of Okuma' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Kan Tan Seiyu Scroll' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Dog Chase Picture' (by Oshima Shokei) ** 'Castle Railing White Rabbit Carving' (by Oshima Shokei) ** 'New Year's Three Cranes Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Landscape Picture' (by Tagu Itsusai) ** 'Scroll by the 3rd lord Kamei Norichika' (inscription: "Love, Everyone suffers the heat, I love the long summer day") ** 'Large plate pottery made by the 7th lord Kamei Norisada' (3 pieces) ** 'Yorokan Academy Plaque' (inscribed by the 8th lord Kamei Norikata) ** 'Ritual Attire of the 11th lord Kamei Noritaka' ** 'Inaba Chronicle' (written in 1861 by Yutaka Tonin) ** 'Scroll of the map of Tsuwano Castle' (by Kurimoto Kakuza) ** 'Kanminto Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei, donated to Emperor Meiji) ** 'Wedding tools of Uesugi Hisako' (5th daughter of Count Uesugi Shigenobu) ** 'First meal tools of Kamei Shuko' (eldest daughter of Count Kamei Naritsune) ** 'Fox statues donated by Princess Tomoko of the Kitashirakawa-no-miya family' ** 'Poem Scroll by Nishi Amane' (poem: "Tanabata fields, east and west of the windblown wheat, blossom") ** 'Nishi Amane Dispersal Sword' (a wooden sword modeled after a kun with an inscription on the back about the legend of Kun) ** 'Nishi Amane thank you letter' (inscription: "Why must people drink green tea, May 14, Meiji 14") ** Other items
* Non-exhibit items ** 'Sakurano-sha Library' (collection of books by Okuma, including 'Nihon Shoki Shiden') ** 'Kusagi Library' (collection of books by Kusagi, a wide range of academic and educational books from the Meiji period) ** 'Saeki Toshimaro Library' (collection of books by Saeki Toshimaro) ** 'Picture of the 33 Kannon Temples of Edo' (by Ogata Gekko) ** 'Landscape Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Saint Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Lu Gong Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Autumn Blood Lu Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Crane and Turtle Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Sanju Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Pine Crane Picture' (by Okano Tozan, first generation of the Okano family, retained by the 3rd lord Norichika) ** 'Eight Views of Omi' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Bird and Flower Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Four Seasons Bird and Flower Screen' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Plum Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Peacock Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Autumn Scenery Picture' (by Kabe Tsunao, student of Ogu Yutaka) ** 'Crane Picture with Autograph' (by Okuno Nagataka) ** 'Yoshiga Record' (written by Ozaki Tasaburo, a chronicle of the Yoshiga and Tsuwano areas) ** 'Permanent Spear' (armory of the domain mansion, with the Kamei family crest) ** Other items
== References == * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Doki, 'New Revised Shinto Dictionary', 1997, Tokyo-do Publishing, ISBN 4-490-10474-X * Tottori City Cultural Foundation, 'Inaba x Toyotomi: Toyotomi Regime and the Daimyo of Inaba', 2019, ISBN 978-4-904099-40-7

It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref> It is an [[Inari shrine]] dedicated to [[Inari Ōkami]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=TOKYO |first=COPYRIGHT © CHUGOKU ＋ SHIKOKU × |title=Taikodani Inari Shrine {{!}} CHUGOKU＋SHIKOKU×TOKYO (JAPAN) |url=https://www.chushikokuandtokyo.org/spot_236/ |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=www.chushikokuandtokyo.org |language=en}}</ref>
It is one of the five great Inari shrines of Japan.<ref name=":0" /><ref>https://zekkeijapan.com/spot/index/1771/</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=日本吉 - NIPPON-KICHI |url=https://nippon-kichi.jp/article_list.do?kwd=3013&ml_lang=en |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=nippon-kichi.jp}}</ref><ref>https://japantravel.navitime.com/en/area/jp/guide/NTJnews0321-en/</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=If you are looking for a sightseeing taxi in Karuizawa, come to “Matsuba Taxi” |url=https://www.matsuba-taxi.com/eng/smart/detail.php?pg=tourism_saku |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=www.matsuba-taxi.com}}</ref>
Kamei Norisada founded it in 1744<ref name=":1" /> [[ja:太皷谷稲成神社]]
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Taikodani Inari Shrine''' (太皷谷稲成神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsuwano Town]], [[Kanoashi District]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is classified as a special shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁) and was formerly designated as a village shrine (郷社). It is commonly referred to as "Tsuwano's Oinari-san" and is sometimes considered one of the [[Three Great Inari Shrines]] of Japan, though it is more commonly recognized as one of the [[Five Great Inari Shrines]]. It ranks second in the number of annual visitors in Shimane Prefecture, following [[Izumo Taisha]].
== History == The shrine was founded on May 15, 1773 (An'ei 2) by the 7th lord of the Tsuwano Domain, Kamei Norisada, who invited deities from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for the prosperity of the domain and its people. The shrine was initially built on the ridge of Taikodani, which is the kimon (northeast) direction of Tsuwano Castle, considered an unlucky direction. In July 1867 (Keiō 3), the Tsuwano Domain conducted a shrine reorganization and moved the Kumano Gongen Shrine, which was enshrined on Otomeyama, to the Taikodani Inari Shrine as an auxiliary deity, renaming it Kumano Shrine. The shrine received reverence from successive lords, and it was not open to the public until after the abolition of feudal domains.
In 1923 (Taishō 12), the shrine pavilion (now the Gongen) was built. In 1924 (Taishō 13), treasures were donated by Princess Tomoko of the Kitashirakawa-no-miya Imperial Family, leading to the establishment of the treasure hall and Yōrō Library. The shrine was renamed back to Taikodani Inari Shrine in 1927 (Shōwa 2), and it was designated as a village shrine in 1935 (Shōwa 10). With the increase in visitors, a new shrine pavilion and main hall were constructed and dedicated in 1969 (Shōwa 44), with the old shrine remaining as the Gongen.
The shrine claims to be one of the Five Great Inari Shrines, alongside [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], [[Takekoma Inari Shrine]], and [[Yutoku Inari Shrine]].
== Shrine Name == The shrine is located in a valley where taiko drums used to sound during the Edo period, giving it the name "Taikodani." The unique spelling "稲成" (instead of the usual "稲荷") symbolizes the granting of wishes. It is the only shrine in Japan to use this particular kanji combination for Inari.
== Deities == * [[Ukanomitama|Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami]] (Inari Ōkami) * [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] (Kumano Ōkami)

The central gate is only used during the New Year and for special festivals. * Main Pavilion and Worship Hall - The new pavilion where it is customary to offer fried tofu. * Rear Worship Hall - Located behind the main pavilion, providing a closer place for worship. * Gongen - The former main pavilion before the new pavilion was built in 1969. * Myōbu Shrine - A subsidiary shrine located behind the Gongen, dedicated to the deity's fox attendants. * Office * Treasure Hall - Houses 88 special treasures, 77 paintings and calligraphy works, 58 pieces of fine art, and 16 other items. * Wedding Hall * Ceremony Hall - A multipurpose hall that can be used for receptions and other events.

* Non-exhibit items ** 'Sakurano-sha Library' (collection of books by Okuma, including 'Nihon Shoki Shiden') ** 'Kusagi Library' (collection of books by Kusagi, a wide range of academic and educational books from the Meiji period) ** 'Saeki Toshimaro Library' (collection of books by Saeki Toshimaro) ** 'Picture of the 33 Kannon Temples of Edo' (by Ogata Gekko) ** 'Landscape Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Saint Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Lu Gong Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Autumn Blood Lu Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Crane and Turtle Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Sanju Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Pine Crane Picture' (by Okano Tozan, first generation of the Okano family, retained by the 3rd lord Norichika) ** 'Eight Views of Omi' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Bird and Flower Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Four Seasons Bird and Flower Screen' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Plum Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Peacock Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Autumn Scenery Picture' (by Kabe Tsunao, student of Ogu Yutaka) ** 'Crane Picture with Autograph' (by Okuno Nagataka) ** 'Yoshiga Record' (written by Ozaki Tasaburo, a chronicle of the Yoshiga and Tsuwano areas) ** 'Permanent Spear' (armory of the domain mansion, with the Kamei family crest) ** Other items

{{infobox shinto shrine| image = Taikodani Inarijinjya haiden.JPG | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }} {{afc comment|1=appears a little notable[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 00:39, 3 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{ill|Taikodani Inari Shrine|ja|太皷谷稲成神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref> It is an [[Inari shrine]] dedicated to [[Inari Ōkami]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=TOKYO |first=COPYRIGHT © CHUGOKU ＋ SHIKOKU × |title=Taikodani Inari Shrine {{!}} CHUGOKU＋SHIKOKU×TOKYO (JAPAN) |url=https://www.chushikokuandtokyo.org/spot_236/ |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=www.chushikokuandtokyo.org |language=en}}</ref>
It is one of the five great Inari shrines of Japan.<ref name=":0" /><ref>https://zekkeijapan.com/spot/index/1771/</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=日本吉 - NIPPON-KICHI |url=https://nippon-kichi.jp/article_list.do?kwd=3013&ml_lang=en |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=nippon-kichi.jp}}</ref><ref>https://japantravel.navitime.com/en/area/jp/guide/NTJnews0321-en/</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=If you are looking for a sightseeing taxi in Karuizawa, come to “Matsuba Taxi” |url=https://www.matsuba-taxi.com/eng/smart/detail.php?pg=tourism_saku |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=www.matsuba-taxi.com}}</ref>
Kamei Norisada founded it in 1744<ref name=":1" /> [[ja:太皷谷稲成神社]]
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Taikodani Inari Shrine''' (太皷谷稲成神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsuwano Town]], [[Kanoashi District]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is classified as a special shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁) and was formerly designated as a village shrine (郷社). It is commonly referred to as "Tsuwano's Oinari-san" and is sometimes considered one of the [[Three Great Inari Shrines]] of Japan, though it is more commonly recognized as one of the [[Five Great Inari Shrines]]. It ranks second in the number of annual visitors in Shimane Prefecture, following [[Izumo Taisha]].
== History == The shrine was founded on May 15, 1773 (An'ei 2) by the 7th lord of the Tsuwano Domain, Kamei Norisada, who invited deities from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for the prosperity of the domain and its people. The shrine was initially built on the ridge of Taikodani, which is the kimon (northeast) direction of Tsuwano Castle, considered an unlucky direction. In July 1867 (Keiō 3), the Tsuwano Domain conducted a shrine reorganization and moved the Kumano Gongen Shrine, which was enshrined on Otomeyama, to the Taikodani Inari Shrine as an auxiliary deity, renaming it Kumano Shrine. The shrine received reverence from successive lords, and it was not open to the public until after the abolition of feudal domains.
In 1923 (Taishō 12), the shrine pavilion (now the Gongen) was built. In 1924 (Taishō 13), treasures were donated by Princess Tomoko of the Kitashirakawa-no-miya Imperial Family, leading to the establishment of the treasure hall and Yōrō Library. The shrine was renamed back to Taikodani Inari Shrine in 1927 (Shōwa 2), and it was designated as a village shrine in 1935 (Shōwa 10). With the increase in visitors, a new shrine pavilion and main hall were constructed and dedicated in 1969 (Shōwa 44), with the old shrine remaining as the Gongen.
The shrine claims to be one of the Five Great Inari Shrines, alongside [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], [[Takekoma Inari Shrine]], and [[Yutoku Inari Shrine]].
== Shrine Name == The shrine is located in a valley where taiko drums used to sound during the Edo period, giving it the name "Taikodani." The unique spelling "稲成" (instead of the usual "稲荷") symbolizes the granting of wishes. It is the only shrine in Japan to use this particular kanji combination for Inari.
== Deities == * [[Ukanomitama|Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami]] (Inari Ōkami) * [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] (Kumano Ōkami)
[[File:View_from_Taikodani_Inari-jinja_shrine,_Tsuwano-cho,_Kanoashi-gun,_Shimane_001.jpg|thumb|500x500px|View of Tsuwano Town from the shrine grounds.]] == Grounds == * Front Approach - The approach consists of 263 stone steps lined with about 1,000 red torii gates. * Chōzuya (Purification Pavilion) * Shrine Gate - A triple gate established in December 1972 (Shōwa 47). The central gate is only used during the New Year and for special festivals. * Main Pavilion and Worship Hall - The new pavilion where it is customary to offer fried tofu. * Rear Worship Hall - Located behind the main pavilion, providing a closer place for worship. * Gongen - The former main pavilion before the new pavilion was built in 1969. * Myōbu Shrine - A subsidiary shrine located behind the Gongen, dedicated to the deity's fox attendants. * Office * Treasure Hall - Houses 88 special treasures, 77 paintings and calligraphy works, 58 pieces of fine art, and 16 other items. * Wedding Hall * Ceremony Hall - A multipurpose hall that can be used for receptions and other events.
<gallery> File:Toriis of Taikodani Inari Shrine 4.jpg|alt=|Front approach with a thousand torii gates File:Shimmon Gate of Taikodani Inari Shrine.jpg|alt=|Shrine gate (approach side) File:Taikodani Inari-jinja.jpg|alt=|Shrine gate (inside grounds) File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Offerings 001.jpg|alt=|Offerings of fried tofu </gallery>
== Subsidiary Shrines == * Gongen ** Deities: Izanami-no-Mikoto (Kumano Ōkami), Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami (Inari Ōkami) :: The former main pavilion before the new pavilion was built in 1969, now serving as the Gongen with a divided spirit enshrined. * Myōbu Shrine ** Deities: Myōbu Senjo - Fox attendants of Inari Ōkami, known as the "Ojou" couple. :: The term "Myōbu" refers to a court lady of the Heian period with a fifth rank or higher, and it is also an epithet for fox attendants of Inari Ōkami.
<gallery> File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Former Shrine 001.jpg|alt=|Gongen File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Myōbu Auxiliary Shrine 001.jpg|alt=|Myōbu Shrine File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Myōbu Auxiliary Shrine 002.jpg|alt=|Myōbu Shrine Pavilion </gallery>
== Festivals == The following festivals are held annually.
Monthly Festivals * 1st and 15th of each month - "Otsuitachi Mairi" and the monthly festival of the former Kumano Shrine.
Annual Festivals * February - First Horse Festival * March - Old First Horse Festival * May 15 - Spring Festival * November 15 - Annual Festival * November 16 - Autumn Festival * November 23 - Harvest Festival
== Cultural Properties == === Shimane Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === ; Old Documents * Celestial Globe and Terrestrial Globe - Designated on May 31, 1966 :: Made by Hori Kinsuke in 1808 (Bunka 5). * Colored maps of Japan's geographical survey and the coastal survey map of the 33 provinces of Eastern Japan - Designated on December 27, 1974 :: Copies of maps surveyed by Inō Tadataka, made by Takahashi Kageyasu.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}
{{infobox shinto shrine| image = Taikodani Inarijinjya haiden.JPG | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }} {{afc comment|1=appears a little notable[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 00:39, 3 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{ill|Taikodani Inari Shrine|ja|太皷谷稲成神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref> It is an [[Inari shrine]] dedicated to [[Inari Ōkami]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=TOKYO |first=COPYRIGHT © CHUGOKU ＋ SHIKOKU × |title=Taikodani Inari Shrine {{!}} CHUGOKU＋SHIKOKU×TOKYO (JAPAN) |url=https://www.chushikokuandtokyo.org/spot_236/ |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=www.chushikokuandtokyo.org |language=en}}</ref>
It is one of the five great Inari shrines of Japan.<ref name=":0" /><ref>https://zekkeijapan.com/spot/index/1771/</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=日本吉 - NIPPON-KICHI |url=https://nippon-kichi.jp/article_list.do?kwd=3013&ml_lang=en |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=nippon-kichi.jp}}</ref><ref>https://japantravel.navitime.com/en/area/jp/guide/NTJnews0321-en/</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=If you are looking for a sightseeing taxi in Karuizawa, come to “Matsuba Taxi” |url=https://www.matsuba-taxi.com/eng/smart/detail.php?pg=tourism_saku |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=www.matsuba-taxi.com}}</ref>
Kamei Norisada founded it in 1744<ref name=":1" /> [[ja:太皷谷稲成神社]]
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Taikodani Inari Shrine''' (太皷谷稲成神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsuwano Town]], [[Kanoashi District]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is classified as a special shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁) and was formerly designated as a village shrine (郷社). It is commonly referred to as "Tsuwano's Oinari-san" and is sometimes considered one of the [[Three Great Inari Shrines]] of Japan, though it is more commonly recognized as one of the [[Five Great Inari Shrines]]. It ranks second in the number of annual visitors in Shimane Prefecture, following [[Izumo Taisha]].
== History == The shrine was founded on May 15, 1773 (An'ei 2) by the 7th lord of the Tsuwano Domain, Kamei Norisada, who invited deities from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for the prosperity of the domain and its people. The shrine was initially built on the ridge of Taikodani, which is the kimon (northeast) direction of Tsuwano Castle, considered an unlucky direction. In July 1867 (Keiō 3), the Tsuwano Domain conducted a shrine reorganization and moved the Kumano Gongen Shrine, which was enshrined on Otomeyama, to the Taikodani Inari Shrine as an auxiliary deity, renaming it Kumano Shrine. The shrine received reverence from successive lords, and it was not open to the public until after the abolition of feudal domains.
In 1923 (Taishō 12), the shrine pavilion (now the Gongen) was built. In 1924 (Taishō 13), treasures were donated by Princess Tomoko of the Kitashirakawa-no-miya Imperial Family, leading to the establishment of the treasure hall and Yōrō Library. The shrine was renamed back to Taikodani Inari Shrine in 1927 (Shōwa 2), and it was designated as a village shrine in 1935 (Shōwa 10). With the increase in visitors, a new shrine pavilion and main hall were constructed and dedicated in 1969 (Shōwa 44), with the old shrine remaining as the Gongen.
The shrine claims to be one of the Five Great Inari Shrines, alongside [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], [[Takekoma Inari Shrine]], and [[Yutoku Inari Shrine]].
== Shrine Name == The shrine is located in a valley where taiko drums used to sound during the Edo period, giving it the name "Taikodani." The unique spelling "稲成" (instead of the usual "稲荷") symbolizes the granting of wishes. It is the only shrine in Japan to use this particular kanji combination for Inari.
== Deities == * [[Ukanomitama|Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami]] (Inari Ōkami) * [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] (Kumano Ōkami)
[[File:View_from_Taikodani_Inari-jinja_shrine,_Tsuwano-cho,_Kanoashi-gun,_Shimane_001.jpg|thumb|500x500px|View of Tsuwano Town from the shrine grounds.]] == Grounds == * Front Approach - The approach consists of 263 stone steps lined with about 1,000 red torii gates. * Chōzuya (Purification Pavilion) * Shrine Gate - A triple gate established in December 1972 (Shōwa 47). The central gate is only used during the New Year and for special festivals. * Main Pavilion and Worship Hall - The new pavilion where it is customary to offer fried tofu. * Rear Worship Hall - Located behind the main pavilion, providing a closer place for worship. * Gongen - The former main pavilion before the new pavilion was built in 1969. * Myōbu Shrine - A subsidiary shrine located behind the Gongen, dedicated to the deity's fox attendants. * Office * Treasure Hall - Houses 88 special treasures, 77 paintings and calligraphy works, 58 pieces of fine art, and 16 other items. * Wedding Hall * Ceremony Hall - A multipurpose hall that can be used for receptions and other events.
<gallery> File:Toriis of Taikodani Inari Shrine 4.jpg|alt=|Front approach with a thousand torii gates File:Shimmon Gate of Taikodani Inari Shrine.jpg|alt=|Shrine gate (approach side) File:Taikodani Inari-jinja.jpg|alt=|Shrine gate (inside grounds) File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Offerings 001.jpg|alt=|Offerings of fried tofu </gallery>
== Subsidiary Shrines == * Gongen ** Deities: Izanami-no-Mikoto (Kumano Ōkami), Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami (Inari Ōkami) :: The former main pavilion before the new pavilion was built in 1969, now serving as the Gongen with a divided spirit enshrined. * Myōbu Shrine ** Deities: Myōbu Senjo - Fox attendants of Inari Ōkami, known as the "Ojou" couple. :: The term "Myōbu" refers to a court lady of the Heian period with a fifth rank or higher, and it is also an epithet for fox attendants of Inari Ōkami.
<gallery> File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Former Shrine 001.jpg|alt=|Gongen File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Myōbu Auxiliary Shrine 001.jpg|alt=|Myōbu Shrine File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Myōbu Auxiliary Shrine 002.jpg|alt=|Myōbu Shrine Pavilion </gallery>
== Festivals == The following festivals are held annually.
Monthly Festivals * 1st and 15th of each month - "Otsuitachi Mairi" and the monthly festival of the former Kumano Shrine.
Annual Festivals * February - First Horse Festival * March - Old First Horse Festival * May 15 - Spring Festival * November 15 - Annual Festival * November 16 - Autumn Festival * November 23 - Harvest Festival
== Cultural Properties == === Shimane Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === ; Old Documents * Celestial Globe and Terrestrial Globe - Designated on May 31, 1966 :: Made by Hori Kinsuke in 1808 (Bunka 5). * Colored maps of Japan's geographical survey and the coastal survey map of the 33 provinces of Eastern Japan - Designated on December 27, 1974 :: Copies of maps surveyed by Inō Tadataka, made by Takahashi Kageyasu.
== Shrine Treasures == The treasure hall houses 88 special treasures, 77 paintings and calligraphy works, 58 pieces of fine art, and 16 other items (currently, armor worn by Kamei Norisada is exhibited at the Kamei Historical Museum).
* Exhibits in the Treasure Hall ** 'Portrait of Kamei Norimasa hunting a tiger' (by Kurimoto Kakuza) ** 'Gift letter for the tiger' (by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) ** 'Four-leaf sakaki crest armor' (worn by retainer Arai Shichibei) ** 'Tiles from Shikano Castle' (bearing the Kamei family crest) ** 'Silkworm Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Portrait of Okuma' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Kan Tan Seiyu Scroll' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Dog Chase Picture' (by Oshima Shokei) ** 'Castle Railing White Rabbit Carving' (by Oshima Shokei) ** 'New Year's Three Cranes Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Landscape Picture' (by Tagu Itsusai) ** 'Scroll by the 3rd lord Kamei Norichika' (inscription: "Love, Everyone suffers the heat, I love the long summer day") ** 'Large plate pottery made by the 7th lord Kamei Norisada' (3 pieces) ** 'Yorokan Academy Plaque' (inscribed by the 8th lord Kamei Norikata) ** 'Ritual Attire of the 11th lord Kamei Noritaka' ** 'Inaba Chronicle' (written in 1861 by Yutaka Tonin) ** 'Scroll of the map of Tsuwano Castle' (by Kurimoto Kakuza) ** 'Kanminto Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei, donated to Emperor Meiji) ** 'Wedding tools of Uesugi Hisako' (5th daughter of Count Uesugi Shigenobu) ** 'First meal tools of Kamei Shuko' (eldest daughter of Count Kamei Naritsune) ** 'Fox statues donated by Princess Tomoko of the Kitashirakawa-no-miya family' ** 'Poem Scroll by Nishi Amane' (poem: "Tanabata fields, east and west of the windblown wheat, blossom") ** 'Nishi Amane Dispersal Sword' (a wooden sword modeled after a kun with an inscription on the back about the legend of Kun) ** 'Nishi Amane thank you letter' (inscription: "Why must people drink green tea, May 14, Meiji 14") ** Other items
* Non-exhibit items ** 'Sakurano-sha Library' (collection of books by Okuma, including 'Nihon Shoki Shiden') ** 'Kusagi Library' (collection of books by Kusagi, a wide range of academic and educational books from the Meiji period) ** 'Saeki Toshimaro Library' (collection of books by Saeki Toshimaro) ** 'Picture of the 33 Kannon Temples of Edo' (by Ogata Gekko) ** 'Landscape Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Saint Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Lu Gong Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Autumn Blood Lu Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Crane and Turtle Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Sanju Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Pine Crane Picture' (by Okano Tozan, first generation of the Okano family, retained by the 3rd lord Norichika) ** 'Eight Views of Omi' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Bird and Flower Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Four Seasons Bird and Flower Screen' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Plum Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Peacock Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Autumn Scenery Picture' (by Kabe Tsunao, student of Ogu Yutaka) ** 'Crane Picture with Autograph' (by Okuno Nagataka) ** 'Yoshiga Record' (written by Ozaki Tasaburo, a chronicle of the Yoshiga and Tsuwano areas) ** 'Permanent Spear' (armory of the domain mansion, with the Kamei family crest) ** Other items
== References == * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Doki, 'New Revised Shinto Dictionary', 1997, Tokyo-do Publishing, ISBN 4-490-10474-X * Tottori City Cultural Foundation, 'Inaba x Toyotomi: Toyotomi Regime and the Daimyo of Inaba', 2019, ISBN 978-4-904099-40-7
== External Links == {{Commonscat}} * {{Official|http://taikodani.jp/}} * [https://twitter.com/taikodaniinari Taikodani Inari Shrine] - Official Twitter * [https://www.instagram.com/taikodani.inari.shrine/ Taikodani Inari Shrine] - Official Instagram * [https://www.kankou-shimane.com/destination/20246 Taikodani Inari Shrine] - Shimane Prefecture Tourism

{{infobox shinto shrine| image = Taikodani Inarijinjya haiden.JPG | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }} {{afc comment|1=appears a little notable[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 00:39, 3 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{ill|Taikodani Inari Shrine|ja|太皷谷稲成神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref> It is an [[Inari shrine]] dedicated to [[Inari Ōkami]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=TOKYO |first=COPYRIGHT © CHUGOKU ＋ SHIKOKU × |title=Taikodani Inari Shrine {{!}} CHUGOKU＋SHIKOKU×TOKYO (JAPAN) |url=https://www.chushikokuandtokyo.org/spot_236/ |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=www.chushikokuandtokyo.org |language=en}}</ref>
It is one of the five great Inari shrines of Japan.<ref name=":0" /><ref>https://zekkeijapan.com/spot/index/1771/</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=日本吉 - NIPPON-KICHI |url=https://nippon-kichi.jp/article_list.do?kwd=3013&ml_lang=en |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=nippon-kichi.jp}}</ref><ref>https://japantravel.navitime.com/en/area/jp/guide/NTJnews0321-en/</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=If you are looking for a sightseeing taxi in Karuizawa, come to “Matsuba Taxi” |url=https://www.matsuba-taxi.com/eng/smart/detail.php?pg=tourism_saku |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=www.matsuba-taxi.com}}</ref>
Kamei Norisada founded it in 1744<ref name=":1" /> [[ja:太皷谷稲成神社]]
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Taikodani Inari Shrine''' (太皷谷稲成神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsuwano Town]], [[Kanoashi District]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is classified as a special shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁) and was formerly designated as a village shrine (郷社). It is commonly referred to as "Tsuwano's Oinari-san" and is sometimes considered one of the [[Three Great Inari Shrines]] of Japan, though it is more commonly recognized as one of the [[Five Great Inari Shrines]]. It ranks second in the number of annual visitors in Shimane Prefecture, following [[Izumo Taisha]].
== History == The shrine was founded on May 15, 1773 (An'ei 2) by the 7th lord of the Tsuwano Domain, Kamei Norisada, who invited deities from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for the prosperity of the domain and its people. The shrine was initially built on the ridge of Taikodani, which is the kimon (northeast) direction of Tsuwano Castle, considered an unlucky direction. In July 1867 (Keiō 3), the Tsuwano Domain conducted a shrine reorganization and moved the Kumano Gongen Shrine, which was enshrined on Otomeyama, to the Taikodani Inari Shrine as an auxiliary deity, renaming it Kumano Shrine. The shrine received reverence from successive lords, and it was not open to the public until after the abolition of feudal domains.
In 1923 (Taishō 12), the shrine pavilion (now the Gongen) was built. In 1924 (Taishō 13), treasures were donated by Princess Tomoko of the Kitashirakawa-no-miya Imperial Family, leading to the establishment of the treasure hall and Yōrō Library. The shrine was renamed back to Taikodani Inari Shrine in 1927 (Shōwa 2), and it was designated as a village shrine in 1935 (Shōwa 10). With the increase in visitors, a new shrine pavilion and main hall were constructed and dedicated in 1969 (Shōwa 44), with the old shrine remaining as the Gongen.
The shrine claims to be one of the Five Great Inari Shrines, alongside [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], [[Takekoma Inari Shrine]], and [[Yutoku Inari Shrine]].
== Shrine Name == The shrine is located in a valley where taiko drums used to sound during the Edo period, giving it the name "Taikodani." The unique spelling "稲成" (instead of the usual "稲荷") symbolizes the granting of wishes. It is the only shrine in Japan to use this particular kanji combination for Inari.
== Deities == * [[Ukanomitama|Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami]] (Inari Ōkami) * [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] (Kumano Ōkami)
[[File:View_from_Taikodani_Inari-jinja_shrine,_Tsuwano-cho,_Kanoashi-gun,_Shimane_001.jpg|thumb|500x500px|View of Tsuwano Town from the shrine grounds.]] == Grounds == * Front Approach - The approach consists of 263 stone steps lined with about 1,000 red torii gates. * Chōzuya (Purification Pavilion) * Shrine Gate - A triple gate established in December 1972 (Shōwa 47). The central gate is only used during the New Year and for special festivals. * Main Pavilion and Worship Hall - The new pavilion where it is customary to offer fried tofu. * Rear Worship Hall - Located behind the main pavilion, providing a closer place for worship. * Gongen - The former main pavilion before the new pavilion was built in 1969. * Myōbu Shrine - A subsidiary shrine located behind the Gongen, dedicated to the deity's fox attendants. * Office * Treasure Hall - Houses 88 special treasures, 77 paintings and calligraphy works, 58 pieces of fine art, and 16 other items. * Wedding Hall * Ceremony Hall - A multipurpose hall that can be used for receptions and other events.
<gallery> File:Toriis of Taikodani Inari Shrine 4.jpg|alt=|Front approach with a thousand torii gates File:Shimmon Gate of Taikodani Inari Shrine.jpg|alt=|Shrine gate (approach side) File:Taikodani Inari-jinja.jpg|alt=|Shrine gate (inside grounds) File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Offerings 001.jpg|alt=|Offerings of fried tofu </gallery>
== Subsidiary Shrines == * Gongen ** Deities: Izanami-no-Mikoto (Kumano Ōkami), Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami (Inari Ōkami) :: The former main pavilion before the new pavilion was built in 1969, now serving as the Gongen with a divided spirit enshrined. * Myōbu Shrine ** Deities: Myōbu Senjo - Fox attendants of Inari Ōkami, known as the "Ojou" couple. :: The term "Myōbu" refers to a court lady of the Heian period with a fifth rank or higher, and it is also an epithet for fox attendants of Inari Ōkami.
<gallery> File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Former Shrine 001.jpg|alt=|Gongen File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Myōbu Auxiliary Shrine 001.jpg|alt=|Myōbu Shrine File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Myōbu Auxiliary Shrine 002.jpg|alt=|Myōbu Shrine Pavilion </gallery>
== Festivals == The following festivals are held annually.
Monthly Festivals * 1st and 15th of each month - "Otsuitachi Mairi" and the monthly festival of the former Kumano Shrine.
Annual Festivals * February - First Horse Festival * March - Old First Horse Festival * May 15 - Spring Festival * November 15 - Annual Festival * November 16 - Autumn Festival * November 23 - Harvest Festival
== Cultural Properties == === Shimane Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === ; Old Documents * Celestial Globe and Terrestrial Globe - Designated on May 31, 1966 :: Made by Hori Kinsuke in 1808 (Bunka 5). * Colored maps of Japan's geographical survey and the coastal survey map of the 33 provinces of Eastern Japan - Designated on December 27, 1974 :: Copies of maps surveyed by Inō Tadataka, made by Takahashi Kageyasu.
== Shrine Treasures == The treasure hall houses 88 special treasures, 77 paintings and calligraphy works, 58 pieces of fine art, and 16 other items (currently, armor worn by Kamei Norisada is exhibited at the Kamei Historical Museum).
* Exhibits in the Treasure Hall ** 'Portrait of Kamei Norimasa hunting a tiger' (by Kurimoto Kakuza) ** 'Gift letter for the tiger' (by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) ** 'Four-leaf sakaki crest armor' (worn by retainer Arai Shichibei) ** 'Tiles from Shikano Castle' (bearing the Kamei family crest) ** 'Silkworm Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Portrait of Okuma' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Kan Tan Seiyu Scroll' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Dog Chase Picture' (by Oshima Shokei) ** 'Castle Railing White Rabbit Carving' (by Oshima Shokei) ** 'New Year's Three Cranes Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Landscape Picture' (by Tagu Itsusai) ** 'Scroll by the 3rd lord Kamei Norichika' (inscription: "Love, Everyone suffers the heat, I love the long summer day") ** 'Large plate pottery made by the 7th lord Kamei Norisada' (3 pieces) ** 'Yorokan Academy Plaque' (inscribed by the 8th lord Kamei Norikata) ** 'Ritual Attire of the 11th lord Kamei Noritaka' ** 'Inaba Chronicle' (written in 1861 by Yutaka Tonin) ** 'Scroll of the map of Tsuwano Castle' (by Kurimoto Kakuza) ** 'Kanminto Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei, donated to Emperor Meiji) ** 'Wedding tools of Uesugi Hisako' (5th daughter of Count Uesugi Shigenobu) ** 'First meal tools of Kamei Shuko' (eldest daughter of Count Kamei Naritsune) ** 'Fox statues donated by Princess Tomoko of the Kitashirakawa-no-miya family' ** 'Poem Scroll by Nishi Amane' (poem: "Tanabata fields, east and west of the windblown wheat, blossom") ** 'Nishi Amane Dispersal Sword' (a wooden sword modeled after a kun with an inscription on the back about the legend of Kun) ** 'Nishi Amane thank you letter' (inscription: "Why must people drink green tea, May 14, Meiji 14") ** Other items
* Non-exhibit items ** 'Sakurano-sha Library' (collection of books by Okuma, including 'Nihon Shoki Shiden') ** 'Kusagi Library' (collection of books by Kusagi, a wide range of academic and educational books from the Meiji period) ** 'Saeki Toshimaro Library' (collection of books by Saeki Toshimaro) ** 'Picture of the 33 Kannon Temples of Edo' (by Ogata Gekko) ** 'Landscape Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Saint Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Lu Gong Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Autumn Blood Lu Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Crane and Turtle Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Sanju Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Pine Crane Picture' (by Okano Tozan, first generation of the Okano family, retained by the 3rd lord Norichika) ** 'Eight Views of Omi' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Bird and Flower Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Four Seasons Bird and Flower Screen' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Plum Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Peacock Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Autumn Scenery Picture' (by Kabe Tsunao, student of Ogu Yutaka) ** 'Crane Picture with Autograph' (by Okuno Nagataka) ** 'Yoshiga Record' (written by Ozaki Tasaburo, a chronicle of the Yoshiga and Tsuwano areas) ** 'Permanent Spear' (armory of the domain mansion, with the Kamei family crest) ** Other items
== References == * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Doki, 'New Revised Shinto Dictionary', 1997, Tokyo-do Publishing, ISBN 4-490-10474-X * Tottori City Cultural Foundation, 'Inaba x Toyotomi: Toyotomi Regime and the Daimyo of Inaba', 2019, ISBN 978-4-904099-40-7

==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Taikodani Inari Shrine''' (太皷谷稲成神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsuwano Town]], [[Kanoashi District]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is classified as a special shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁) and was formerly designated as a village shrine (郷社). It is commonly referred to as "Tsuwano's Oinari-san" and is sometimes considered one of the [[Three Great Inari Shrines]] of Japan, though it is more commonly recognized as one of the [[Five Great Inari Shrines]]. It ranks second in the number of annual visitors in Shimane Prefecture, following [[Izumo Taisha]].
== History == The shrine was founded on May 15, 1773 (An'ei 2) by the 7th lord of the Tsuwano Domain, Kamei Norisada, who invited deities from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for the prosperity of the domain and its people. The shrine was initially built on the ridge of Taikodani, which is the kimon (northeast) direction of Tsuwano Castle, considered an unlucky direction. In July 1867 (Keiō 3), the Tsuwano Domain conducted a shrine reorganization and moved the Kumano Gongen Shrine, which was enshrined on Otomeyama, to the Taikodani Inari Shrine as an auxiliary deity, renaming it Kumano Shrine. The shrine received reverence from successive lords, and it was not open to the public until after the abolition of feudal domains.
In 1923 (Taishō 12), the shrine pavilion (now the Gongen) was built. In 1924 (Taishō 13), treasures were donated by Princess Tomoko of the Kitashirakawa-no-miya Imperial Family, leading to the establishment of the treasure hall and Yōrō Library. The shrine was renamed back to Taikodani Inari Shrine in 1927 (Shōwa 2), and it was designated as a village shrine in 1935 (Shōwa 10). With the increase in visitors, a new shrine pavilion and main hall were constructed and dedicated in 1969 (Shōwa 44), with the old shrine remaining as the Gongen.
The shrine claims to be one of the Five Great Inari Shrines, alongside [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], [[Takekoma Inari Shrine]], and [[Yutoku Inari Shrine]].
== Shrine Name == The shrine is located in a valley where taiko drums used to sound during the Edo period, giving it the name "Taikodani." The unique spelling "稲成" (instead of the usual "稲荷") symbolizes the granting of wishes. It is the only shrine in Japan to use this particular kanji combination for Inari.
== Deities == * [[Ukanomitama|Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami]] (Inari Ōkami) * [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] (Kumano Ōkami)
[[File:View_from_Taikodani_Inari-jinja_shrine,_Tsuwano-cho,_Kanoashi-gun,_Shimane_001.jpg|thumb|500x500px|View of Tsuwano Town from the shrine grounds.]] == Grounds == * Front Approach - The approach consists of 263 stone steps lined with about 1,000 red torii gates. * Chōzuya (Purification Pavilion) * Shrine Gate - A triple gate established in December 1972 (Shōwa 47). The central gate is only used during the New Year and for special festivals. * Main Pavilion and Worship Hall - The new pavilion where it is customary to offer fried tofu. * Rear Worship Hall - Located behind the main pavilion, providing a closer place for worship. * Gongen - The former main pavilion before the new pavilion was built in 1969. * Myōbu Shrine - A subsidiary shrine located behind the Gongen, dedicated to the deity's fox attendants. * Office * Treasure Hall - Houses 88 special treasures, 77 paintings and calligraphy works, 58 pieces of fine art, and 16 other items. * Wedding Hall * Ceremony Hall - A multipurpose hall that can be used for receptions and other events.
<gallery> File:Toriis of Taikodani Inari Shrine 4.jpg|alt=|Front approach with a thousand torii gates File:Shimmon Gate of Taikodani Inari Shrine.jpg|alt=|Shrine gate (approach side) File:Taikodani Inari-jinja.jpg|alt=|Shrine gate (inside grounds) File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Offerings 001.jpg|alt=|Offerings of fried tofu </gallery>
== Subsidiary Shrines == * Gongen ** Deities: Izanami-no-Mikoto (Kumano Ōkami), Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami (Inari Ōkami) :: The former main pavilion before the new pavilion was built in 1969, now serving as the Gongen with a divided spirit enshrined. * Myōbu Shrine ** Deities: Myōbu Senjo - Fox attendants of Inari Ōkami, known as the "Ojou" couple. :: The term "Myōbu" refers to a court lady of the Heian period with a fifth rank or higher, and it is also an epithet for fox attendants of Inari Ōkami.
<gallery> File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Former Shrine 001.jpg|alt=|Gongen File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Myōbu Auxiliary Shrine 001.jpg|alt=|Myōbu Shrine File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Myōbu Auxiliary Shrine 002.jpg|alt=|Myōbu Shrine Pavilion </gallery>
== Festivals == The following festivals are held annually.
Monthly Festivals * 1st and 15th of each month - "Otsuitachi Mairi" and the monthly festival of the former Kumano Shrine.
Annual Festivals * February - First Horse Festival * March - Old First Horse Festival * May 15 - Spring Festival * November 15 - Annual Festival * November 16 - Autumn Festival * November 23 - Harvest Festival
== Cultural Properties == === Shimane Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === ; Old Documents * Celestial Globe and Terrestrial Globe - Designated on May 31, 1966 :: Made by Hori Kinsuke in 1808 (Bunka 5). * Colored maps of Japan's geographical survey and the coastal survey map of the 33 provinces of Eastern Japan - Designated on December 27, 1974 :: Copies of maps surveyed by Inō Tadataka, made by Takahashi Kageyasu.
== Shrine Treasures == The treasure hall houses 88 special treasures, 77 paintings and calligraphy works, 58 pieces of fine art, and 16 other items (currently, armor worn by Kamei Norisada is exhibited at the Kamei Historical Museum).
* Exhibits in the Treasure Hall ** 'Portrait of Kamei Norimasa hunting a tiger' (by Kurimoto Kakuza) ** 'Gift letter for the tiger' (by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) ** 'Four-leaf sakaki crest armor' (worn by retainer Arai Shichibei) ** 'Tiles from Shikano Castle' (bearing the Kamei family crest) ** 'Silkworm Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Portrait of Okuma' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Kan Tan Seiyu Scroll' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Dog Chase Picture' (by Oshima Shokei) ** 'Castle Railing White Rabbit Carving' (by Oshima Shokei) ** 'New Year's Three Cranes Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Landscape Picture' (by Tagu Itsusai) ** 'Scroll by the 3rd lord Kamei Norichika' (inscription: "Love, Everyone suffers the heat, I love the long summer day") ** 'Large plate pottery made by the 7th lord Kamei Norisada' (3 pieces) ** 'Yorokan Academy Plaque' (inscribed by the 8th lord Kamei Norikata) ** 'Ritual Attire of the 11th lord Kamei Noritaka' ** 'Inaba Chronicle' (written in 1861 by Yutaka Tonin) ** 'Scroll of the map of Tsuwano Castle' (by Kurimoto Kakuza) ** 'Kanminto Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei, donated to Emperor Meiji) ** 'Wedding tools of Uesugi Hisako' (5th daughter of Count Uesugi Shigenobu) ** 'First meal tools of Kamei Shuko' (eldest daughter of Count Kamei Naritsune) ** 'Fox statues donated by Princess Tomoko of the Kitashirakawa-no-miya family' ** 'Poem Scroll by Nishi Amane' (poem: "Tanabata fields, east and west of the windblown wheat, blossom") ** 'Nishi Amane Dispersal Sword' (a wooden sword modeled after a kun with an inscription on the back about the legend of Kun) ** 'Nishi Amane thank you letter' (inscription: "Why must people drink green tea, May 14, Meiji 14") ** Other items
* Non-exhibit items ** 'Sakurano-sha Library' (collection of books by Okuma, including 'Nihon Shoki Shiden') ** 'Kusagi Library' (collection of books by Kusagi, a wide range of academic and educational books from the Meiji period) ** 'Saeki Toshimaro Library' (collection of books by Saeki Toshimaro) ** 'Picture of the 33 Kannon Temples of Edo' (by Ogata Gekko) ** 'Landscape Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Saint Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Lu Gong Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Autumn Blood Lu Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Crane and Turtle Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Sanju Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Pine Crane Picture' (by Okano Tozan, first generation of the Okano family, retained by the 3rd lord Norichika) ** 'Eight Views of Omi' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Bird and Flower Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Four Seasons Bird and Flower Screen' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Plum Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Peacock Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Autumn Scenery Picture' (by Kabe Tsunao, student of Ogu Yutaka) ** 'Crane Picture with Autograph' (by Okuno Nagataka) ** 'Yoshiga Record' (written by Ozaki Tasaburo, a chronicle of the Yoshiga and Tsuwano areas) ** 'Permanent Spear' (armory of the domain mansion, with the Kamei family crest) ** Other items
== References == * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Doki, 'New Revised Shinto Dictionary', 1997, Tokyo-do Publishing, ISBN 4-490-10474-X * Tottori City Cultural Foundation, 'Inaba x Toyotomi: Toyotomi Regime and the Daimyo of Inaba', 2019, ISBN 978-4-904099-40-7

==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Taikodani Inari Shrine''' (太皷谷稲成神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsuwano Town]], [[Kanoashi District]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is classified as a special shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁) and was formerly designated as a village shrine (郷社). It is commonly referred to as "Tsuwano's Oinari-san" and is sometimes considered one of the [[Three Great Inari Shrines]] of Japan, though it is more commonly recognized as one of the [[Five Great Inari Shrines]]. It ranks second in the number of annual visitors in Shimane Prefecture, following [[Izumo Taisha]].
== History == The shrine was founded on May 15, 1773 (An'ei 2) by the 7th lord of the Tsuwano Domain, Kamei Norisada, who invited deities from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for the prosperity of the domain and its people. The shrine was initially built on the ridge of Taikodani, which is the kimon (northeast) direction of Tsuwano Castle, considered an unlucky direction. In July 1867 (Keiō 3), the Tsuwano Domain conducted a shrine reorganization and moved the Kumano Gongen Shrine, which was enshrined on Otomeyama, to the Taikodani Inari Shrine as an auxiliary deity, renaming it Kumano Shrine. The shrine received reverence from successive lords, and it was not open to the public until after the abolition of feudal domains.
In 1923 (Taishō 12), the shrine pavilion (now the Gongen) was built. In 1924 (Taishō 13), treasures were donated by Princess Tomoko of the Kitashirakawa-no-miya Imperial Family, leading to the establishment of the treasure hall and Yōrō Library. The shrine was renamed back to Taikodani Inari Shrine in 1927 (Shōwa 2), and it was designated as a village shrine in 1935 (Shōwa 10). With the increase in visitors, a new shrine pavilion and main hall were constructed and dedicated in 1969 (Shōwa 44), with the old shrine remaining as the Gongen.
The shrine claims to be one of the Five Great Inari Shrines, alongside [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], [[Takekoma Inari Shrine]], and [[Yutoku Inari Shrine]].
== Shrine Name == The shrine is located in a valley where taiko drums used to sound during the Edo period, giving it the name "Taikodani." The unique spelling "稲成" (instead of the usual "稲荷") symbolizes the granting of wishes. It is the only shrine in Japan to use this particular kanji combination for Inari.
== Deities == * [[Ukanomitama|Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami]] (Inari Ōkami) * [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] (Kumano Ōkami)

The central gate is only used during the New Year and for special festivals. * Main Pavilion and Worship Hall - The new pavilion where it is customary to offer fried tofu. * Rear Worship Hall - Located behind the main pavilion, providing a closer place for worship. * Gongen - The former main pavilion before the new pavilion was built in 1969. * Myōbu Shrine - A subsidiary shrine located behind the Gongen, dedicated to the deity's fox attendants. * Office * Treasure Hall - Houses 88 special treasures, 77 paintings and calligraphy works, 58 pieces of fine art, and 16 other items. * Wedding Hall * Ceremony Hall - A multipurpose hall that can be used for receptions and other events.

== Cultural Properties == === Shimane Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === ; Old Documents * Celestial Globe and Terrestrial Globe - Designated on May 31, 1966 :: Made by Hori Kinsuke in 1808 (Bunka 5). * Colored maps of Japan's geographical survey and the coastal survey map of the 33 provinces of Eastern Japan - Designated on December 27, 1974 :: Copies of maps surveyed by Inō Tadataka, made by Takahashi Kageyasu.
== Shrine Treasures == The treasure hall houses 88 special treasures, 77 paintings and calligraphy works, 58 pieces of fine art, and 16 other items (currently, armor worn by Kamei Norisada is exhibited at the Kamei Historical Museum).
* Exhibits in the Treasure Hall ** 'Portrait of Kamei Norimasa hunting a tiger' (by Kurimoto Kakuza) ** 'Gift letter for the tiger' (by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) ** 'Four-leaf sakaki crest armor' (worn by retainer Arai Shichibei) ** 'Tiles from Shikano Castle' (bearing the Kamei family crest) ** 'Silkworm Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Portrait of Okuma' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Kan Tan Seiyu Scroll' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Dog Chase Picture' (by Oshima Shokei) ** 'Castle Railing White Rabbit Carving' (by Oshima Shokei) ** 'New Year's Three Cranes Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Landscape Picture' (by Tagu Itsusai) ** 'Scroll by the 3rd lord Kamei Norichika' (inscription: "Love, Everyone suffers the heat, I love the long summer day") ** 'Large plate pottery made by the 7th lord Kamei Norisada' (3 pieces) ** 'Yorokan Academy Plaque' (inscribed by the 8th lord Kamei Norikata) ** 'Ritual Attire of the 11th lord Kamei Noritaka' ** 'Inaba Chronicle' (written in 1861 by Yutaka Tonin) ** 'Scroll of the map of Tsuwano Castle' (by Kurimoto Kakuza) ** 'Kanminto Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei, donated to Emperor Meiji) ** 'Wedding tools of Uesugi Hisako' (5th daughter of Count Uesugi Shigenobu) ** 'First meal tools of Kamei Shuko' (eldest daughter of Count Kamei Naritsune) ** 'Fox statues donated by Princess Tomoko of the Kitashirakawa-no-miya family' ** 'Poem Scroll by Nishi Amane' (poem: "Tanabata fields, east and west of the windblown wheat, blossom") ** 'Nishi Amane Dispersal Sword' (a wooden sword modeled after a kun with an inscription on the back about the legend of Kun) ** 'Nishi Amane thank you letter' (inscription: "Why must people drink green tea, May 14, Meiji 14") ** Other items
* Non-exhibit items ** 'Sakurano-sha Library' (collection of books by Okuma, including 'Nihon Shoki Shiden') ** 'Kusagi Library' (collection of books by Kusagi, a wide range of academic and educational books from the Meiji period) ** 'Saeki Toshimaro Library' (collection of books by Saeki Toshimaro) ** 'Picture of the 33 Kannon Temples of Edo' (by Ogata Gekko) ** 'Landscape Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Saint Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Lu Gong Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Autumn Blood Lu Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Crane and Turtle Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Sanju Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Pine Crane Picture' (by Okano Tozan, first generation of the Okano family, retained by the 3rd lord Norichika) ** 'Eight Views of Omi' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Bird and Flower Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Four Seasons Bird and Flower Screen' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Plum Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Peacock Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Autumn Scenery Picture' (by Kabe Tsunao, student of Ogu Yutaka) ** 'Crane Picture with Autograph' (by Okuno Nagataka) ** 'Yoshiga Record' (written by Ozaki Tasaburo, a chronicle of the Yoshiga and Tsuwano areas) ** 'Permanent Spear' (armory of the domain mansion, with the Kamei family crest) ** Other items

It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref> It is an [[Inari shrine]] dedicated to [[Inari Ōkami]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=TOKYO |first=COPYRIGHT © CHUGOKU ＋ SHIKOKU × |title=Taikodani Inari Shrine {{!}} CHUGOKU＋SHIKOKU×TOKYO (JAPAN) |url=https://www.chushikokuandtokyo.org/spot_236/ |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=www.chushikokuandtokyo.org |language=en}}</ref>
It is one of the five great Inari shrines of Japan.<ref name=":0" /><ref>https://zekkeijapan.com/spot/index/1771/</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=日本吉 - NIPPON-KICHI |url=https://nippon-kichi.jp/article_list.do?kwd=3013&ml_lang=en |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=nippon-kichi.jp}}</ref><ref>https://japantravel.navitime.com/en/area/jp/guide/NTJnews0321-en/</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=If you are looking for a sightseeing taxi in Karuizawa, come to “Matsuba Taxi” |url=https://www.matsuba-taxi.com/eng/smart/detail.php?pg=tourism_saku |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=www.matsuba-taxi.com}}</ref>
Kamei Norisada founded it in 1744<ref name=":1" /> [[ja:太皷谷稲成神社]]
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Taikodani Inari Shrine''' (太皷谷稲成神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsuwano Town]], [[Kanoashi District]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is classified as a special shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁) and was formerly designated as a village shrine (郷社). It is commonly referred to as "Tsuwano's Oinari-san" and is sometimes considered one of the [[Three Great Inari Shrines]] of Japan, though it is more commonly recognized as one of the [[Five Great Inari Shrines]]. It ranks second in the number of annual visitors in Shimane Prefecture, following [[Izumo Taisha]].
== History == The shrine was founded on May 15, 1773 (An'ei 2) by the 7th lord of the Tsuwano Domain, Kamei Norisada, who invited deities from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for the prosperity of the domain and its people. The shrine was initially built on the ridge of Taikodani, which is the kimon (northeast) direction of Tsuwano Castle, considered an unlucky direction. In July 1867 (Keiō 3), the Tsuwano Domain conducted a shrine reorganization and moved the Kumano Gongen Shrine, which was enshrined on Otomeyama, to the Taikodani Inari Shrine as an auxiliary deity, renaming it Kumano Shrine. The shrine received reverence from successive lords, and it was not open to the public until after the abolition of feudal domains.
In 1923 (Taishō 12), the shrine pavilion (now the Gongen) was built. In 1924 (Taishō 13), treasures were donated by Princess Tomoko of the Kitashirakawa-no-miya Imperial Family, leading to the establishment of the treasure hall and Yōrō Library. The shrine was renamed back to Taikodani Inari Shrine in 1927 (Shōwa 2), and it was designated as a village shrine in 1935 (Shōwa 10). With the increase in visitors, a new shrine pavilion and main hall were constructed and dedicated in 1969 (Shōwa 44), with the old shrine remaining as the Gongen.
The shrine claims to be one of the Five Great Inari Shrines, alongside [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], [[Takekoma Inari Shrine]], and [[Yutoku Inari Shrine]].
== Shrine Name == The shrine is located in a valley where taiko drums used to sound during the Edo period, giving it the name "Taikodani." The unique spelling "稲成" (instead of the usual "稲荷") symbolizes the granting of wishes. It is the only shrine in Japan to use this particular kanji combination for Inari.
== Deities == * [[Ukanomitama|Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami]] (Inari Ōkami) * [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] (Kumano Ōkami)
[[File:View_from_Taikodani_Inari-jinja_shrine,_Tsuwano-cho,_Kanoashi-gun,_Shimane_001.jpg|thumb|500x500px|View of Tsuwano Town from the shrine grounds.]] == Grounds == * Front Approach - The approach consists of 263 stone steps lined with about 1,000 red torii gates. * Chōzuya (Purification Pavilion) * Shrine Gate - A triple gate established in December 1972 (Shōwa 47). The central gate is only used during the New Year and for special festivals. * Main Pavilion and Worship Hall - The new pavilion where it is customary to offer fried tofu. * Rear Worship Hall - Located behind the main pavilion, providing a closer place for worship. * Gongen - The former main pavilion before the new pavilion was built in 1969. * Myōbu Shrine - A subsidiary shrine located behind the Gongen, dedicated to the deity's fox attendants. * Office * Treasure Hall - Houses 88 special treasures, 77 paintings and calligraphy works, 58 pieces of fine art, and 16 other items. * Wedding Hall * Ceremony Hall - A multipurpose hall that can be used for receptions and other events.
<gallery> File:Toriis of Taikodani Inari Shrine 4.jpg|alt=|Front approach with a thousand torii gates File:Shimmon Gate of Taikodani Inari Shrine.jpg|alt=|Shrine gate (approach side) File:Taikodani Inari-jinja.jpg|alt=|Shrine gate (inside grounds) File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Offerings 001.jpg|alt=|Offerings of fried tofu </gallery>
== Subsidiary Shrines == * Gongen ** Deities: Izanami-no-Mikoto (Kumano Ōkami), Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami (Inari Ōkami) :: The former main pavilion before the new pavilion was built in 1969, now serving as the Gongen with a divided spirit enshrined. * Myōbu Shrine ** Deities: Myōbu Senjo - Fox attendants of Inari Ōkami, known as the "Ojou" couple. :: The term "Myōbu" refers to a court lady of the Heian period with a fifth rank or higher, and it is also an epithet for fox attendants of Inari Ōkami.
<gallery> File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Former Shrine 001.jpg|alt=|Gongen File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Myōbu Auxiliary Shrine 001.jpg|alt=|Myōbu Shrine File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Myōbu Auxiliary Shrine 002.jpg|alt=|Myōbu Shrine Pavilion </gallery>
== Festivals == The following festivals are held annually.
Monthly Festivals * 1st and 15th of each month - "Otsuitachi Mairi" and the monthly festival of the former Kumano Shrine.
Annual Festivals * February - First Horse Festival * March - Old First Horse Festival * May 15 - Spring Festival * November 15 - Annual Festival * November 16 - Autumn Festival * November 23 - Harvest Festival

{{infobox shinto shrine| image = Taikodani Inarijinjya haiden.JPG | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }} {{afc comment|1=appears a little notable[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 00:39, 3 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{ill|Taikodani Inari Shrine|ja|太皷谷稲成神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref> It is an [[Inari shrine]] dedicated to [[Inari Ōkami]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=TOKYO |first=COPYRIGHT © CHUGOKU ＋ SHIKOKU × |title=Taikodani Inari Shrine {{!}} CHUGOKU＋SHIKOKU×TOKYO (JAPAN) |url=https://www.chushikokuandtokyo.org/spot_236/ |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=www.chushikokuandtokyo.org |language=en}}</ref>
It is one of the five great Inari shrines of Japan.<ref name=":0" /><ref>https://zekkeijapan.com/spot/index/1771/</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=日本吉 - NIPPON-KICHI |url=https://nippon-kichi.jp/article_list.do?kwd=3013&ml_lang=en |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=nippon-kichi.jp}}</ref><ref>https://japantravel.navitime.com/en/area/jp/guide/NTJnews0321-en/</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=If you are looking for a sightseeing taxi in Karuizawa, come to “Matsuba Taxi” |url=https://www.matsuba-taxi.com/eng/smart/detail.php?pg=tourism_saku |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=www.matsuba-taxi.com}}</ref>
Kamei Norisada founded it in 1744<ref name=":1" /> [[ja:太皷谷稲成神社]]
==Overview==
[[File:Noda-jinja Yamaguchi Trees and Torii.jpg|thumb|300px|Pathway Entrance]] '''Taikodani Inari Shrine''' (太皷谷稲成神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tsuwano Town]], [[Kanoashi District]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is classified as a special shrine under the Association of Shinto Shrines (神社本庁) and was formerly designated as a village shrine (郷社). It is commonly referred to as "Tsuwano's Oinari-san" and is sometimes considered one of the [[Three Great Inari Shrines]] of Japan, though it is more commonly recognized as one of the [[Five Great Inari Shrines]]. It ranks second in the number of annual visitors in Shimane Prefecture, following [[Izumo Taisha]].
== History == The shrine was founded on May 15, 1773 (An'ei 2) by the 7th lord of the Tsuwano Domain, Kamei Norisada, who invited deities from [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]] in Kyoto to pray for the prosperity of the domain and its people. The shrine was initially built on the ridge of Taikodani, which is the kimon (northeast) direction of Tsuwano Castle, considered an unlucky direction. In July 1867 (Keiō 3), the Tsuwano Domain conducted a shrine reorganization and moved the Kumano Gongen Shrine, which was enshrined on Otomeyama, to the Taikodani Inari Shrine as an auxiliary deity, renaming it Kumano Shrine. The shrine received reverence from successive lords, and it was not open to the public until after the abolition of feudal domains.
In 1923 (Taishō 12), the shrine pavilion (now the Gongen) was built. In 1924 (Taishō 13), treasures were donated by Princess Tomoko of the Kitashirakawa-no-miya Imperial Family, leading to the establishment of the treasure hall and Yōrō Library. The shrine was renamed back to Taikodani Inari Shrine in 1927 (Shōwa 2), and it was designated as a village shrine in 1935 (Shōwa 10). With the increase in visitors, a new shrine pavilion and main hall were constructed and dedicated in 1969 (Shōwa 44), with the old shrine remaining as the Gongen.
The shrine claims to be one of the Five Great Inari Shrines, alongside [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], [[Takekoma Inari Shrine]], and [[Yutoku Inari Shrine]].
== Shrine Name == The shrine is located in a valley where taiko drums used to sound during the Edo period, giving it the name "Taikodani." The unique spelling "稲成" (instead of the usual "稲荷") symbolizes the granting of wishes. It is the only shrine in Japan to use this particular kanji combination for Inari.
== Deities == * [[Ukanomitama|Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami]] (Inari Ōkami) * [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] (Kumano Ōkami)
[[File:View_from_Taikodani_Inari-jinja_shrine,_Tsuwano-cho,_Kanoashi-gun,_Shimane_001.jpg|thumb|500x500px|View of Tsuwano Town from the shrine grounds.]] == Grounds == * Front Approach - The approach consists of 263 stone steps lined with about 1,000 red torii gates. * Chōzuya (Purification Pavilion) * Shrine Gate - A triple gate established in December 1972 (Shōwa 47). The central gate is only used during the New Year and for special festivals. * Main Pavilion and Worship Hall - The new pavilion where it is customary to offer fried tofu. * Rear Worship Hall - Located behind the main pavilion, providing a closer place for worship. * Gongen - The former main pavilion before the new pavilion was built in 1969. * Myōbu Shrine - A subsidiary shrine located behind the Gongen, dedicated to the deity's fox attendants. * Office * Treasure Hall - Houses 88 special treasures, 77 paintings and calligraphy works, 58 pieces of fine art, and 16 other items. * Wedding Hall * Ceremony Hall - A multipurpose hall that can be used for receptions and other events.
<gallery> File:Toriis of Taikodani Inari Shrine 4.jpg|alt=|Front approach with a thousand torii gates File:Shimmon Gate of Taikodani Inari Shrine.jpg|alt=|Shrine gate (approach side) File:Taikodani Inari-jinja.jpg|alt=|Shrine gate (inside grounds) File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Offerings 001.jpg|alt=|Offerings of fried tofu </gallery>
== Subsidiary Shrines == * Gongen ** Deities: Izanami-no-Mikoto (Kumano Ōkami), Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami (Inari Ōkami) :: The former main pavilion before the new pavilion was built in 1969, now serving as the Gongen with a divided spirit enshrined. * Myōbu Shrine ** Deities: Myōbu Senjo - Fox attendants of Inari Ōkami, known as the "Ojou" couple. :: The term "Myōbu" refers to a court lady of the Heian period with a fifth rank or higher, and it is also an epithet for fox attendants of Inari Ōkami.
<gallery> File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Former Shrine 001.jpg|alt=|Gongen File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Myōbu Auxiliary Shrine 001.jpg|alt=|Myōbu Shrine File:Taikodani Inari-jinja Myōbu Auxiliary Shrine 002.jpg|alt=|Myōbu Shrine Pavilion </gallery>
== Festivals == The following festivals are held annually.
Monthly Festivals * 1st and 15th of each month - "Otsuitachi Mairi" and the monthly festival of the former Kumano Shrine.
Annual Festivals * February - First Horse Festival * March - Old First Horse Festival * May 15 - Spring Festival * November 15 - Annual Festival * November 16 - Autumn Festival * November 23 - Harvest Festival
== Cultural Properties == === Shimane Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === ; Old Documents * Celestial Globe and Terrestrial Globe - Designated on May 31, 1966 :: Made by Hori Kinsuke in 1808 (Bunka 5). * Colored maps of Japan's geographical survey and the coastal survey map of the 33 provinces of Eastern Japan - Designated on December 27, 1974 :: Copies of maps surveyed by Inō Tadataka, made by Takahashi Kageyasu.
== Shrine Treasures == The treasure hall houses 88 special treasures, 77 paintings and calligraphy works, 58 pieces of fine art, and 16 other items (currently, armor worn by Kamei Norisada is exhibited at the Kamei Historical Museum).
* Exhibits in the Treasure Hall ** 'Portrait of Kamei Norimasa hunting a tiger' (by Kurimoto Kakuza) ** 'Gift letter for the tiger' (by Toyotomi Hideyoshi) ** 'Four-leaf sakaki crest armor' (worn by retainer Arai Shichibei) ** 'Tiles from Shikano Castle' (bearing the Kamei family crest) ** 'Silkworm Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Portrait of Okuma' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Kan Tan Seiyu Scroll' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Dog Chase Picture' (by Oshima Shokei) ** 'Castle Railing White Rabbit Carving' (by Oshima Shokei) ** 'New Year's Three Cranes Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Landscape Picture' (by Tagu Itsusai) ** 'Scroll by the 3rd lord Kamei Norichika' (inscription: "Love, Everyone suffers the heat, I love the long summer day") ** 'Large plate pottery made by the 7th lord Kamei Norisada' (3 pieces) ** 'Yorokan Academy Plaque' (inscribed by the 8th lord Kamei Norikata) ** 'Ritual Attire of the 11th lord Kamei Noritaka' ** 'Inaba Chronicle' (written in 1861 by Yutaka Tonin) ** 'Scroll of the map of Tsuwano Castle' (by Kurimoto Kakuza) ** 'Kanminto Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei, donated to Emperor Meiji) ** 'Wedding tools of Uesugi Hisako' (5th daughter of Count Uesugi Shigenobu) ** 'First meal tools of Kamei Shuko' (eldest daughter of Count Kamei Naritsune) ** 'Fox statues donated by Princess Tomoko of the Kitashirakawa-no-miya family' ** 'Poem Scroll by Nishi Amane' (poem: "Tanabata fields, east and west of the windblown wheat, blossom") ** 'Nishi Amane Dispersal Sword' (a wooden sword modeled after a kun with an inscription on the back about the legend of Kun) ** 'Nishi Amane thank you letter' (inscription: "Why must people drink green tea, May 14, Meiji 14") ** Other items
* Non-exhibit items ** 'Sakurano-sha Library' (collection of books by Okuma, including 'Nihon Shoki Shiden') ** 'Kusagi Library' (collection of books by Kusagi, a wide range of academic and educational books from the Meiji period) ** 'Saeki Toshimaro Library' (collection of books by Saeki Toshimaro) ** 'Picture of the 33 Kannon Temples of Edo' (by Ogata Gekko) ** 'Landscape Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Saint Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Lu Gong Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Autumn Blood Lu Picture' (by Yamamoto Shokei) ** 'Crane and Turtle Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Sanju Picture' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Pine Crane Picture' (by Okano Tozan, first generation of the Okano family, retained by the 3rd lord Norichika) ** 'Eight Views of Omi' (by Okano Tozan) ** 'Bird and Flower Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Four Seasons Bird and Flower Screen' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Plum Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Peacock Picture' (by Miura Shiwan) ** 'Autumn Scenery Picture' (by Kabe Tsunao, student of Ogu Yutaka) ** 'Crane Picture with Autograph' (by Okuno Nagataka) ** 'Yoshiga Record' (written by Ozaki Tasaburo, a chronicle of the Yoshiga and Tsuwano areas) ** 'Permanent Spear' (armory of the domain mansion, with the Kamei family crest) ** Other items
== References == * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Doki, 'New Revised Shinto Dictionary', 1997, Tokyo-do Publishing, ISBN 4-490-10474-X * Tottori City Cultural Foundation, 'Inaba x Toyotomi: Toyotomi Regime and the Daimyo of Inaba', 2019, ISBN 978-4-904099-40-7
